blocks.spoken: Uh, what else? So, um, we adjusted the constructor. Update your custom DBContext constructor, Example, public class Product, public long InternalId { get; set; }, public Guid PublicId { get; set; }, public string Name { get; set; }, public int LifeSpan { get; set; }, public bool IsSystem { get; set; }, public string IsolationId { get; set; }, New work item, public class ProductConfiguration : IEntityTypeConfiguration<Product>, public void Configure(EntityTypeBuilder<Product> builder), builder.HasKey(x => x.InternalId);, builder.Property(x => x.InternalId).ValueGeneratedOnAdd();, builder.Property(x => x.PublicId).IsRequired();, builder.Property(x => x.Name).IsRequired();, builder.Property(x => x.LifeSpan).IsRequired();, builder.Property(x => x.IsSystem).IsRequired();, builder.HasIsolation(x => x.IsolationId).SetIsolationLevel(IsolationLevel.Internal);, 5. Update middleware registration in Program.Startups, builder.Services.AddBusinessIsolationComponent<BusinessIsolationContext, BusinessIsolationProvider, BusinessIsolationComponent>();, builder.Services.AddBusinessIsolationComponent<BusinessIsolationContext, BusinessIsolationProvider, BusinessIsolationComponent>(options => options.SetIsolationLevel(IsolationLevel.MasterInstance));

segment_summary: This segment demonstrates the migration process from version 1 to version 2 of an isolation component, focusing on updating the DBContext constructor and middleware registration. The speaker explains how to replace attribute-based isolation with a fluent API approach and shows that the migration generates an empty file, indicating no schema changes are needed due to existing V1 configurations. The segment concludes with a Q&A session where the speaker clarifies that master instances will represent country-specific data and discusses future plans for test data and documentation.
